Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Carl Stumpf

1848 – 1936

Experimental phenomenology: the psychology of sound and the discipline of describing experience.

Carl Stumpf’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a realist, descriptive-functional, anti-reductionist psychology of consciousness, integrated with an empirically disciplined metaphysics. His central claim is that consciousness is not adequately described as a bundle of sensations, nor as the manifestation of an immediately intuited ego-substance. The given field of experience contains at least three irreducible factors: phenomena or appearances, psychical functions or mental acts, and relations. The mind is primarily the law-governed life of functions: noticing, perceiving, presenting, abstracting, judging, feeling, desiring, and willing. At the metaphysical level, Stumpf rejects phenomenalism, neutral monism, and psychophysical parallelism, and he provisionally favors a form of interactionist dualism; but at the descriptive-psychological level he refuses to make the soul-substance the starting point of psychology. This makes his view subtler than a standard Cartesian substance dualism and much richer than a sensationist or behaviorist psychology.

Stumpf stands at a historically decisive point. He was trained by Brentano and Lotze, supervised Husserl’s habilitation, received the dedication of Husserl’s Logical Investigations, directed the Berlin Institute of Psychology, influenced the emergence of Gestalt psychology, and founded the Berlin Phonogram Archive. He is therefore one of the key mediating figures between Brentano’s act psychology, nineteenth-century experimental psychology, Husserlian phenomenology, Gestalt theory, and later non-reductive approaches to mind.

1. Stumpf’s governing project: philosophy through psychology, but not psychologism

Stumpf’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his general conception of philosophy. He wanted philosophy to be empirical without becoming positivist, and scientific without surrendering its metaphysical and normative ambitions. His program is often described as a “philosophy of experience”: philosophy must work with the sciences, especially psychology, but it must also clarify the most general concepts, laws, and categories that the special sciences presuppose.

This is why psychology has a privileged role. For Stumpf, psychology is not merely one empirical discipline among others; it is the connecting discipline between logic, ethics, aesthetics, pedagogy, law, and the human sciences generally. But psychology does not replace these disciplines. It studies the origins, structures, and laws of mental functions; it does not by itself justify logical truth, ethical validity, or aesthetic value. Stumpf repeatedly distinguishes the psychological origin of concepts from the epistemological or logical justification of truths. That distinction is central to his anti-psychologism.

His methodological model is therefore neither armchair rationalism nor crude experimentalism. Stumpf valued laboratory work, especially in acoustics, music psychology, perception, and child psychology, but he denied that external experiment alone could resolve the fundamental problems of mind. Experiment must be joined to conceptual analysis, descriptive psychology, and metaphysics. In his Berlin laboratory, philosophy remained closely tied to experimental practice, and Stumpf’s own students included figures central to Gestalt psychology.

The result is a distinctive structure:

First, phenomenology studies sensory phenomena and their structural relations.

Second, descriptive psychology studies psychical functions: perceiving, judging, feeling, willing, and so forth.

Third, eidology studies the “formations” or products of functions, such as concepts, states of affairs, and values.

Fourth, metaphysics or “metapsychics” attempts, after the sciences, to understand the relation between mind, body, nature, and reality as a whole.

This architecture is crucial: Stumpf is not simply doing empirical psychology, and he is not simply doing speculative metaphysics. He is trying to build a philosophy of mind in which empirical description, experimental evidence, logical clarification, and metaphysical hypothesis are coordinated.

2. The immediately given: phenomena, functions, and relations

Stumpf’s basic ontology of experience begins with the distinction between Erscheinungen, usually translated as appearances or phenomena, and psychische Funktionen, psychical or mental functions.

Phenomena are the contents of sensation and presentation: colors, tones, spatial extensions, temporal durations, tactile qualities, remembered or imagined sensory contents, and the like. Stumpf distinguishes first-order phenomena, such as actually sensed tones or colors, from second-order phenomena, such as remembered or merely represented tones and colors. These phenomena are not physical objects; they are the immediately given sensory contents through which objects may be known, inferred, or constructed.

Psychical functions are the acts, processes, states, or experiences in which a subject notices, perceives, compares, combines, abstracts, judges, feels, desires, and wills. Stumpf explicitly includes intellectual functions and affective-volitional functions: noticing, judging, concept-forming, feeling, desiring, and willing are all functions. The term “function” does not mean a mere causal output or behavior; it means the lived mental activity itself.

Relations are also fundamental. Stumpf holds that relations among phenomena are not simply invented by the mind after the fact. Similarity, equality, difference, part-whole structure, dependency, fusion, and other relations can be given “with” the phenomena. They are not themselves psychical acts, but they are apprehended by psychical acts and belong to the material with which thought works.

This gives Stumpf a threefold account of experience:

Phenomena are what is sensed or presented.

Functions are the acts or states through which phenomena are noticed, grasped, judged, valued, or willed.

Relations are structural connections that can be directly apprehended among phenomena or among functions.

Stumpf insists that the totality of what is immediately given is real. Phenomena are real as contents to which functions relate; functions are real as acts directed toward or operating upon contents; relations are real as relations. He rejects the idea that phenomena are “mere appearances” in the sense of being nothing at all, but he also rejects the naïve identification of phenomena with independent physical things. Phenomena and functions form a real unity in consciousness, yet they remain categorially distinct.

This distinction is one of the deepest commitments of Stumpf’s philosophy of mind. Consciousness is not made of sensations alone. A judgment is not a sound, color, or tactile datum; an act of noticing is not itself blue or loud; a desire is not a tone; an affirmation is not a spatial figure. Mental functions are not reducible to phenomenal contents. Conversely, phenomena are not reducible to the fact that they are perceived. A tone is not internally constituted by the predicate “being heard,” even though in experience it is given as heard.

This position is anti-phenomenalist and anti-Machian. Against Ernst Mach’s tendency to reduce both physical and psychical reality to neutral complexes of sensations, Stumpf argues that functions have their own mode of givenness and their own laws. Against Berkeleyan idealism, he argues that it is not contradictory to think of appearances without their being currently perceived, even if we first know them in perception. Stumpf even says that the facts present a kind of “double face,” and that in this respect later philosophers have not simply overcome Descartes’s dualism.

But the duality is not a simple Cartesian split between two independently known substances. It is, first, a descriptive distinction within the immediately given: phenomena and functions are actually intertwined, but conceptually and logically distinguishable. Stumpf repeatedly emphasizes that they form a unity in experience and are separable only by abstraction.

3. Functional psychology: mind as act, not container

Stumpf’s psychology is often called functional psychology, but that term must be handled carefully. It does not mean twentieth-century American functionalism in the sense of adaptation-oriented psychology, nor contemporary computational functionalism. For Stumpf, functions are conscious acts or states. The mind is not first a container of mental images; it is a field of active and passive functions.

The most primitive function is Bemerken, noticing or taking note. Sensation, in the psychologically relevant sense, is not bare sensory stimulation; it is the noticing of first-order phenomena. Presentation is the noticing of second-order phenomena, such as remembered or imagined colors and tones. Perception is therefore already functional: it involves the mind’s taking note of parts, wholes, and relations.

This is especially important for attention. Stumpf argues that a change in what is noticed need not be a change in the phenomenal content itself. A tone in a chord can become salient without the chord’s sensory content changing. Similarly, one may notice a taste, a pressure, or an element in a complex field that was previously unnoticed. The difference lies in the function of noticing, not necessarily in the sensory phenomenon.

This gives Stumpf a sophisticated alternative to two inadequate models. Against sensationism, he denies that all mental differences are differences among sensory contents. Against intellectualism, he denies that perception is already judgment in the full logical sense. Between raw sensation and explicit judgment lies a rich functional structure: noticing, attending, comparing, grouping, abstracting, and synthesizing.

Stumpf classifies mental functions broadly into intellectual and emotional-volitional functions. Intellectual functions include perception, presentation, abstraction, synthesis, concept formation, and judgment. Emotional and volitional functions include feelings, emotions, desire, and will. These are not isolated compartments. Judgments can found emotions; values can become objects of desire; concepts and presentations can structure practical willing.

The upshot is that Stumpf’s “mind” is not a mysterious inner substance first known independently of its acts. The mind is disclosed through the organized life of functions. This makes his psychology act-centered, dynamic, and intentional.

4. Intentionality, judgment, and the objectivity of mental content

Stumpf inherits from Brentano the basic act/content orientation, but he modifies it in important ways. For Brentano, intentionality is the mark of the mental: mental acts are directed toward objects. Stumpf keeps the act/content structure, but he develops a more elaborate theory of contents, relations, and products of mental functions.

Judgment is especially important. Stumpf analyzes judgment in terms of quality and matter. The quality is the affirmative or negative stance: accepting, rejecting, affirming, denying. The matter is the complex of presentations or contents to which that stance applies. From acts of judgment arise objective formations, especially Sachverhalte, states of affairs. A state of affairs is not simply a mental image, but neither is it a Platonic object wholly independent of acts. It is an objective correlate or product of judging that can be studied in its own right.

This gives Stumpf a middle position between psychologism and Platonism. Concepts, states of affairs, values, and similar “formations” are dependent on mental acts for their genesis, but once formed they have an objectivity that makes them available for logic, mathematics, ethics, aesthetics, and science. They are not private mental episodes. They can be shared, compared, analyzed, and evaluated.

This is one of Stumpf’s major contributions to the later development of philosophy of mind and logic. The idea that judgment has an objective correlate in a state of affairs anticipates central themes in Husserl, Meinong, early analytic philosophy, and later theories of propositional content. Stumpf’s view also helps explain how psychology can study acts without reducing truth to psychology. Acts produce or disclose contents, but truth is not merely the occurrence of an act.

5. Stumpf’s phenomenology: sensory, experimental, and pre-transcendental

Stumpf’s use of the word “phenomenology” must not be confused with Husserl’s later transcendental phenomenology. Stumpfian phenomenology is not primarily a doctrine of transcendental subjectivity, pure ego, or epoché. It is a neutral preparatory science of sensory phenomena and their structural laws: colors, tones, spatial and temporal sensory forms, and relations such as similarity, difference, and fusion.

Stumpf’s phenomenology is therefore closer to a rigorous science of sensory appearance than to later transcendental phenomenology. It can be experimental. It asks how tones combine, how sensory qualities vary, how relations are given, and what structural features phenomena have before they are interpreted as physical objects or psychological acts. Stumpf explicitly says that much of his work was phenomenological preparation, but that his ultimate aim was to understand functions.

The difference from Husserl is historically important. Husserl admired Stumpf and dedicated the Logical Investigations to him, but Stumpf later criticized Husserl’s pure or transcendental phenomenology, especially the doctrine of the pure ego. Stumpf’s complaint was that Husserl’s later phenomenology risked becoming a “phenomenology without phenomena.”

The contrast is revealing. Husserl radicalizes the analysis of intentional consciousness and transcendental constitution. Stumpf remains more realist, more tied to experimental science, and more suspicious of a pure ego detached from empirical functions. For Stumpf, the road to mind runs through the careful description of actual mental functions in relation to sensory contents, not through a transcendental purification of consciousness.

6. Tonpsychologie and the philosophy of auditory mind

Stumpf’s Tonpsychologie is not a side project. It is the empirical center of his philosophy of mind. Auditory experience gives him a domain in which sensory phenomena, relations, attention, judgment, and musical form can be studied with unusual precision. In the preface to the second volume, Stumpf says that psychology is an “outwork” of philosophy, and that sensory perception is an outwork of psychology; even his painstaking work with tones, pipes, reeds, and tuning forks is ultimately connected to metaphysics and ethics.

A key concept is Verschmelzung, tonal fusion. Fusion is the relation between two sensory contents, especially tones, by which they form not a mere sum but a whole. The more fused the tones are, the more their total impression approaches a single unified sensory impression, and the harder they are to analyze into separate components. Stumpf emphasizes that fusion is not a theoretical fiction invented to solve a problem; it is a sensory relation that must be heard and compared.

Fusion is not a process and not a third tone-quality added to the two tones. It is a relation between the tones themselves. Stumpf also avoids saying that two tones literally become one tone. They remain distinguishable, but their relation can be one of greater or lesser unity.

This matters philosophically. Fusion shows that sensory consciousness is not atomistic. The mind does not first receive isolated sensory atoms and then externally associate them. Some relations of wholeness are already present in the phenomenal field. Stumpf’s hierarchy of fusion among intervals, with the octave showing especially strong fusion and other intervals showing lesser degrees, becomes an empirical basis for a non-associationist theory of perceptual organization.

This is one of the ways Stumpf prepares Gestalt psychology. The later Gestaltists would reject atomistic sensation psychology and emphasize organized wholes. Stumpf did not found Gestalt psychology as a school, but his Berlin institute and his work on fusion, phenomenal relations, and wholeness helped create the conceptual and institutional conditions for it.

7. Perception, attention, and the non-atomistic mind

Stumpf’s account of perception is one of the strongest parts of his philosophy of mind. He rejects the idea that perception is a passive reception of sensory atoms. But he also rejects the idea that perception is simply judgment. Perception is a layered process involving phenomenal content, noticing, attention, relation-apprehension, and sometimes judgment.

The primitive act is noticing. When a subject hears a chord, the tones may be phenomenally present even if not all are explicitly noticed. When one tone becomes noticed, the change is not necessarily a change in the sensory material; it may be a change in function. Stumpf uses such examples to show that the mind’s active organization cannot be reduced to phenomenal intensity or sensory composition.

This is an important anticipation of later philosophy of attention. Stumpf distinguishes the existence of a sensory content from the functional status of being noticed. He thereby avoids a common confusion: treating every difference in awareness as a difference in sensation. In contemporary terms, Stumpf is sensitive to the distinction between phenomenal presence, attentional salience, and cognitive judgment.

His view also supports a realist account of perception. Because phenomena and functions are distinct, Stumpf can say that appearances are given in consciousness without reducing them to acts of consciousness. Because physical objects are not identical with sensory phenomena, he can treat the external world as a scientifically warranted hypothesis or construction rather than as a mere bundle of sensations. This is why his position is usually described as a form of critical realism.

8. Emotion, value, and will

Stumpf’s philosophy of mind is not limited to perception and judgment. His treatment of emotion is especially significant because he rejects the reduction of emotion to bodily sensation or affective tone.

He distinguishes sense-feelings from emotions. Sense-feelings include bodily pleasure and pain and sensory-affective qualities associated with colors, sounds, and other sensory phenomena. Emotions, by contrast, are intentional states such as joy, sadness, anger, disgust, or indignation. They are directed toward objects or, more precisely in many cases, toward states of affairs.

This distinction allows Stumpf to criticize both sensation-based theories of emotion and overly intellectualist theories. An emotion is not merely a sensation in the body, but it is also not a detached judgment. It is an affective-evaluative function founded on cognition. For example, joy or grief is not merely a sensory feeling; it involves a state of affairs apprehended as good, bad, fortunate, regrettable, admirable, or hateful.

Stumpf also distinguishes passive and active feelings. Passive feelings are directed toward existing objects or states of affairs. Active feelings relate to duties, values, desire, and will. Values are therefore not reducible to sensations; they are contents or correlates of affective and volitional functions. Just as judgment has the polarity of affirmation and negation, feeling has positive and negative orientations such as love and hate, attraction and aversion.

This gives Stumpf a cognitive-evaluative theory of emotion. Emotions are not blind inner episodes; they are intentional, value-laden, and often founded upon judgments. This is historically important because it places Stumpf in a line that runs from Brentano’s intentional theory of mind to later phenomenological and analytic theories of emotion as world-directed and evaluative.

9. The soul, the self, and incorporeal mind

Stumpf’s remarks on the soul are subtle and must be handled with care. He does not build psychology upon an immediately intuited ego-substance. In Erscheinungen und psychische Funktionen, he explicitly says that functional psychology is not necessarily ego psychology. Awareness of psychical functions is not the same as awareness of a hidden substance behind those functions.

At the descriptive-psychological level, Stumpf is willing to identify the “soul” with the whole of psychical functions and dispositions. That is, the soul can be understood as the organized unity of mental acts, states, capacities, tendencies, and dispositions. If one adds an unperceived constant substrate behind this whole, that substrate is an inference, not an immediate datum. Stumpf’s rule is clear: what is immediately evident as fact must be perceivable in some way; a hidden soul-substance is not given in the same way functions are given.

This means Stumpf is not a simple Cartesian introspectionist. He does not begin with “I think” as the intuition of an immaterial ego. Nor does he think psychology needs a metaphysical soul-substance in order to describe mental life. In this respect, his functional psychology participates in the nineteenth-century movement away from a psychology grounded in the traditional doctrine of the soul.

But Stumpf is also not a materialist. Psychical functions do not have the predicates of sensory phenomena. A judging, noticing, or willing is not extended in space, colored, loud, or spatially shaped. Stumpf even argues that no predicate of the world of appearances, except perhaps temporality, applies straightforwardly to psychical functions. Mental functions have their own features: clarity, evidence, directedness, generality, motivational connection, and affective force.

The best reconstruction is therefore this: Stumpf is a descriptive functional actualist about the soul, but a metaphysical interactionist dualist about mind and body. Descriptively, the soul is not an immediately intuited substance; it is the unity of functions and dispositions. Metaphysically, the psychical is not reducible to the physical or phenomenal, and the relation between body and mind is not one of mere parallelism.

On incorporeal minds specifically, the evidence is more limited. Stumpf gives us an account of psychical functions as non-physical in the sense that they are not spatial sensory phenomena. He also allows that a substance behind functions could be inferred rather than immediately perceived. But the core sources do not show him developing a robust doctrine of disembodied personal minds or immortal incorporeal souls. His main concern is not to prove the independent existence of incorporeal persons; it is to show that mental functions are irreducible, immediately given, and causally significant. That is the safer and more rigorous conclusion.

10. Mind and body: against parallelism, neutral monism, and reduction

Stumpf’s metaphysical psychology includes a direct engagement with the mind-body problem. He rejects psychophysical parallelism, the view that mental and bodily events run in lawful correspondence without causal interaction. He also rejects Machian phenomenalism or neutral monism, which reduces both the physical and the psychical to complexes of sensations or neutral elements.

Following Lotze, Stumpf provisionally favors interactionism. Bodily processes can causally affect psychical processes, and psychical processes can causally affect bodily processes. He treats this not as an immediately given datum in the same sense as a mental function, but as a metaphysical hypothesis grounded in experience, science, and reflection. His broader metaphysics is inductive and continuous with empirical knowledge, not an a priori construction.

Stumpf’s interactionism is not a crude dualism of two alien substances. He wants to preserve the unity of the world while also preserving the irreducibility of the psychical. In his Erkenntnislehre, he even suggests that the much-discredited dualism may appear, from a deeper standpoint, as a truer monism because the world is a unified organic whole. The point is not that mind and body are identical, but that their interaction belongs to one world-order.

His use of Darwinian evolution against parallelism is also important. If consciousness, judgment, feeling, and will have no causal efficacy, it becomes difficult to understand their role in an evolved organism. Stumpf’s interactionism lets mental functions matter: perception, valuation, desire, and will are not idle shadows of physiology.

This makes Stumpf historically significant for philosophy of mind. He is neither a Cartesian substance dualist in the old introspective sense, nor a materialist, nor a phenomenalist, nor a parallelist. His mature position is a non-reductive, realist, interactionist account of mental functions.

11. Theology, pantheism, and the larger metaphysical horizon

Stumpf’s metaphysics also includes theological reflection. He rejected traditional theism largely because of the problem of evil and adopted a form of pantheism influenced by Spinoza. He interpreted Spinoza’s parallelism in an unusual way, not primarily as psychophysical parallelism, but as something closer to an act/content or attribute relation.

This matters for his philosophy of mind because it shows that his rejection of reductionism does not lead him back to a simple Christian or Cartesian soul metaphysics. His metaphysical horizon is more Spinozistic and Lotzean: the world is unified, mind and nature are deeply connected, and psychical functions are irreducible aspects of reality. But again, this is not a detailed doctrine of disembodied personal minds. It is a metaphysical framework for understanding the place of mind in the world.

12. Anti-psychologism: why Stumpf’s psychology does not destroy objectivity

A central difficulty for any psychology-centered philosophy is psychologism: the reduction of logic, mathematics, truth, or value to mental processes. Stumpf avoids this by distinguishing between three things: the genesis of concepts, the occurrence of judgments, and the validity of truths.

Concepts such as number, space, causality, and relation may originate psychologically in experience. But the truths expressed in mathematics, logic, and epistemology are not thereby empirical generalizations about how people happen to think. Stumpf rejects both Mill’s empiricist reduction of necessary truths and Kant’s transcendental idealist solution. He treats many axioms as analytic or a priori while still holding that the concepts involved can arise from experience.

This is a powerful synthesis. Psychology studies the acts through which concepts are formed and judgments occur. Logic and epistemology study the validity of judgments and the structure of truth. Eidology studies the formations that arise from acts: concepts, states of affairs, values. Metaphysics studies the most general structure of reality. These disciplines are connected, but not reducible to one another.

In contemporary terms, Stumpf is neither a naturalized epistemologist in the strong reductionist sense nor a transcendental philosopher who brackets empirical psychology. He thinks philosophy must pass the test of psychology, but psychology does not legislate truth.

13. Historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Stumpf’s position can be located through a set of contrasts.

Against Descartes, Stumpf denies that psychology should begin from an immediately intuited ego-substance. Yet he preserves a duality between psychical functions and sensory-physical phenomena.

Against Humean and Machian phenomenalism, he denies that the mind is a bundle or complex of sensations. Functions are immediately given and irreducible.

Against Kantian and neo-Kantian idealism, he ties the categories and structures of thought to experience, perception, and psychological development, while still preserving a priori validity where appropriate.

Against Fechnerian or Spinozistic psychophysical parallelism, at least as commonly understood in the nineteenth century, he argues for causal interaction between body and mind.

Against Husserl’s later transcendental phenomenology, he keeps phenomenology tied to sensory phenomena and empirical science rather than pure consciousness or the transcendental ego.

Against associationist atomism, he emphasizes relations, wholes, fusion, and structural organization in sensory experience.

Against later behaviorism, though he predates its dominance, he would insist that mental functions are directly describable and not reducible to publicly observable behavior.

Against later reductive physicalism, he would insist that psychical functions have their own categorial features and cannot be translated into physical or phenomenal predicates.

His positive historical role is that of a bridge. He carries Brentano’s act psychology into experimental psychology; he helps prepare Husserlian phenomenology while refusing Husserl’s transcendental turn; he helps prepare Gestalt psychology through his work on wholes, relations, and tonal fusion; and he contributes to the anti-psychologistic clarification of judgment, content, and state of affairs.

14. A systematic reconstruction: Stumpf’s philosophy of mind in theses

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Stumpf can be formulated as follows.

1. The given is not homogeneous.
Immediate experience contains sensory phenomena, psychical functions, and relations. Any theory that reduces all three to sensations, physical processes, or hidden substances falsifies the structure of experience.

2. Mentality is functional.
The mind is disclosed through acts and states: noticing, perceiving, presenting, comparing, abstracting, judging, feeling, desiring, and willing. These functions are not theoretical posits but immediately describable features of consciousness.

3. Phenomena and functions are actually unified but conceptually distinct.
In lived consciousness, one does not find bare phenomena without functions or bare functions without contents. But the two are not identical. The tone heard is not the hearing; the judgment made is not a color or tone; the desire is not a sensory image.

4. Perception is structured by noticing and attention.
Perception is not passive reception. A content may be present without being saliently noticed, and a change in attention need not be a change in sensory material. This makes Stumpf a precursor to later theories of attention and perceptual organization.

5. Sensory consciousness is relational and holistic.
Relations such as similarity, difference, part-whole structure, dependency, and fusion are not arbitrary mental additions. In tonal fusion especially, Stumpf shows that phenomenal contents can form wholes that are not mere sums.

6. Judgment has objective correlates.
Judging is an act with affirmative or negative quality and determinate matter. Its product or correlate is a state of affairs. This lets Stumpf explain objectivity without reducing logic to psychology or positing a separate Platonic realm.

7. Emotions are intentional and evaluative.
Emotions are not merely bodily sensations or pleasures and pains. They are directed toward states of affairs and structured by value. Desire and will are likewise value-directed functions.

8. The soul is not the starting point of psychology.
Psychology does not require an immediately given soul-substance. The soul may be understood descriptively as the whole of functions and dispositions. A deeper substrate, if posited, is an inference, not an introspective datum.

9. Mind is irreducible to body, but body and mind interact.
Stumpf rejects both reductionism and psychophysical parallelism. His mature metaphysical preference is interactionism: bodily and psychical processes causally affect one another within one unified world-order.

10. Philosophy of mind must be empirical, descriptive, and metaphysical.
The right method is neither pure speculation nor mere experimentation. It requires descriptive psychology, phenomenological analysis of sensory contents, experimental discipline, logical clarification, and metaphysical synthesis.

15. Tensions and unresolved problems

Stumpf’s position is powerful, but it is not without tensions.

The first tension concerns the soul. Descriptively, Stumpf avoids the soul-substance and treats the soul as the unity of functions and dispositions. Metaphysically, however, he accepts interactionist dualism and leaves room for an inferred substrate. The resulting view is deliberately cautious, but it can look unstable: is the subject merely the unity of functions, or is there a deeper bearer of those functions?

The second tension concerns interactionism. Stumpf rejects parallelism and reductionism, but he does not give a fully developed mechanism of mind-body causation. His interactionism is an empirically motivated metaphysical hypothesis, not a completed causal theory. This leaves him exposed to later worries about causal closure, conservation laws, and the explanatory relation between physiology and conscious agency.

The third tension concerns the status of formations such as concepts, values, and states of affairs. Stumpf wants them to be act-dependent enough to avoid Platonism, but objective enough to avoid psychologism. This middle position is attractive, but delicate. It requires a careful account of how something can be produced or disclosed by acts while remaining valid beyond any individual act.

The fourth tension concerns phenomenology. Stumpf’s phenomenology is sensory and empirical, which gives it scientific discipline. But this also limits it compared with Husserl’s more radical analysis of intentional consciousness, temporality, selfhood, and constitution. Stumpf’s refusal of the transcendental turn is philosophically principled, but it narrows the kind of phenomenological questions he can ask.

Still, these tensions are not failures so much as signs of Stumpf’s historical location. He is working at the point where psychology is becoming experimental, phenomenology is becoming a philosophical method, logic is trying to free itself from psychologism, and the mind-body problem is being reconfigured by physiology and evolutionary theory.

Final characterization

Stumpf’s philosophy of mind may be summarized as follows: the mind is the irreducible, law-governed, intentional-functional side of experience, inseparably joined with phenomena in consciousness but not reducible to them; it is embodied and causally efficacious, yet not physical; it can be studied empirically without being reduced to physiology, and it can be treated metaphysically without beginning from an immediately intuited soul-substance.

Historically, Stumpf belongs among the major architects of non-reductive philosophy of mind. His importance lies not in founding a single school, but in holding together themes that later became separated: Brentano’s act psychology, rigorous sensory phenomenology, experimental method, anti-psychologistic logic, Gestalt holism, intentional emotion theory, and interactionist metaphysics. His thought gives us a philosophy of mind in which perception, judgment, affect, value, will, body, and world are parts of one systematic inquiry rather than isolated problems.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
